claim_text: We used the fastMRI multi-coil knee dataset with sparse and parallel reconstruction.[42]

sub_claims.text: knoll2020 is the fastMRI dataset paper introducing a multi-coil knee dataset usable for sparse/parallel reconstruction research.

evidence.snippet: fastMRI: A Publicly Available Raw k-Space and DICOM Dataset of Knee Images for Accelerated MR Image Reconstruction Using Machine Learning

The purpose of the fastMRI dataset is to provide the first step toward addressing this issue. ... Our dataset includes both raw MRI k-space data and magnitude Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM) images. The k-space data comprises 1594 measurement datasets obtained in knee MRI examinations from a range of MRI systems and clinical patient populations...

The total size of the k-space data is approximately 1.35 TB. It is split up into the following files for download: multicoil_train (931 GB), multicoil_val (192 GB), multi-coil_test (109 GB), singlecoil_train (88 GB), singlecoil_val (19 GB), and singlecoil_test (7 GB).

Because the data were acquired with a multichannel receive array coil, a proper combination of the individual coil images is a necessary step in the image reconstruction process. ... Image reconstruction of accelerated acquisitions via parallel imaging requires an additional calibration step to obtain coil sensitivity information.

Examples in the test and challenge sets contain undersampled k-space data. The undersampling is performed by retrospectively masking k-space lines from a fully sampled acquisition. ... For the challenge and test set, unique examples are provided for the single-coil and the multicoil dataset.

sub_claims.nuance: Source explicitly introduces the fastMRI multi-coil knee dataset (multicoil_train/val/test partitions, 1594 fully-sampled multichannel knee acquisitions) and discusses parallel-imaging / accelerated (sparse, undersampled) reconstruction as the dataset's core use case. This is the canonical citation for the dataset itself (distinct from C045, where knoll2020 is mis-cited for an architecture spec).

attestation.indirect_attribution_check: Source is the primary dataset descriptor paper (Knoll et al., Radiology: AI 2020, doi:10.1148/ryai.2020190007); no indirect attribution issue.
attestation.out_of_context_check: Manuscript uses knoll2020 to cite the fastMRI multi-coil knee dataset itself, which matches the source's purpose verbatim. The 'sparse and parallel reconstruction' framing aligns with the paper's stated motivation: undersampled (sparse) acquisitions reconstructed via parallel imaging.
